entry.title: Current vs Non-Current Deferred Revenue Handling in EBRR – Standard Capabilities in PublicCloud 2602
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have a requirement to classify deferred revenue generated via Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) into current and non-current portions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

In this context, we have the following queries:

  1. Reclassification to Separate G/L Accounts
    In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, is there any standard functionality to reclassify EBRR-managed deferred revenue into current and non-current G/L accounts at period end (e.g., via automated postings), without requiring manual journal entries on EBRR-managed accounts?

  2. Standard Reporting for Current vs Non-Current Split
    Is there any standard analytical report that provides a current vs non-current classification of EBRR-generated deferred revenue balances across:

    • Sales Orders
    • Projects
    • Service Documents

As we are not using Contract-Based Revenue Recognition, the standard report “Contract Balance Reclassification” is not applicable in our scenario.

Appreciate your guidance on standard capabilities or recommended approaches in Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Is It Okay to Use Only RECN Instead of ABST for Recon...
entry.content:
Hi Minsung_Lee,

To answer your question directly: From my perspective, there are no critical system integration or long-term issues when using exclusively the RECN account group instead of ABST.

The difference between ABST and RECN in the standard SAP pre-delivered Chart of Accounts (YCOA) is not a hard architectural restriction, but rather a matter of Master Data Governance (Field Status configuration).

Here is the factual breakdown of the impact (check SAP Help Account Groups )

1. The Difference: Field Status In S/4HANA Cloud, Account Groups primarily control number ranges and the Field Status of G/L master data.

  • ABST is configured to hide the "Recon. Account Ready for Input" flag.

  • RECN is configured to make the "Recon. Account Ready for Input" flag optional/visible in the company code specific data.

If you assign all reconciliation accounts to RECN but leave the "Ready for Input" checkbox unticked, the accounts will behave exactly like standard ABST accounts.

2. Impact on your specific concerns:

  • Integration issues between subledger (S/L) and general ledger (G/L): None. Setting an account to RECN and even checking the "Ready for Input" flag does not open the account to direct FI journal entries. Because the account is still defined as a reconciliation account (for Customers or Suppliers), S/4HANA will strictly block direct manual postings (e.g., via "Post General Journal Entries"). All postings must still flow through the subledger, meaning S/L and G/L balances will remain perfectly aligned.

  • Restrictions when using Alternative Reconciliation Account: Enabler, not a restriction. Using RECN is actually a prerequisite for using Alternative Reconciliation Accounts. If a business process requires a user to manually change the reconciliation account during document entry (e.g., in "Create Incoming Invoices"), the targeted G/L account must be in the RECN group with the "Ready for Input" flag checked. Check Sap Note 2844132 

     

  • Impact on Special G/L Indicator processing: None. Special G/L transactions (like Down Payments) use their own separate logic for account determination (Automatic Account determination app.). The system looks at this configuration for Special G/L indicators to find the target alternative reconciliation account. Even RECN Account Group opens the alternative reconciliation account filed during manual posting, you can not save a different target account for the same reconciliation account in the configuration. Therefore, there is no effect as well.

  • Effects on standard reports or analytics tools: Minimal (Process-dependent). The system standard reporting remains unaffected. The only potential "pitfall" is human error. If you enable the "Ready for Input" flag on all RECN accounts, users might accidentally change the reconciliation account manually during regular invoice posting when they shouldn't. This won't break subledger integration, but it could misclassify balances on your Trial Balance (e.g., classifying standard trade payables under a different liability bucket).

Conclusion: Using only RECN is completely fine and will not break the system. However, from a strict Master Data Governance perspective, it is best practice to keep the "Ready for Input" flag unticked unless the specific account explicitly requires manual alternative reconciliation postings.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Serial number converting in to special character
entry.content:
Since we cannot directly access your system, we recommend the following steps to diagnose and resolve the issue of serial numbers being converted to special characters during upload:

  • Verify & Correct Excel File Encoding 
    Excel files ( .xlsx, .xls) may use different encodings (UTF-8, ANSI, etc.), which can lead to data corruption when processed by SAP. To mitigate this, save fime in utf-8 (or the appropriate encoding for your region)..
  • Confirm SAP System Encoding Settings
    SAP may misinterpret input data due to incorrect system-level encoding configurations. To ensure proper handling: Ensure the system is configured to UTF-8 (or the appropriate encoding for your region).
  • Additional Checks (Method-Dependent)
    Further troubleshooting steps depend on the upload method

entry.title: Re: SAP DRC - Electronic Invoicing for Italy : ItalyReceiveNotification without SNI
entry.content: Explanation I receive : The Italian Revenue Agency's *Sistema di Interscambio* (SdI) client does not send the SNI extension during the TLS handshake. When the SdI makes the `ItalyReceiveNotification` call to SAP BTP, the suite expects the host name indication (SNI) during the TLS handshake. Because it is not received, the SAP BTP load balancer or Ingress controller rejects the connection or fails to route it correctly at the TLS level, resulting in an internal HTTP 500 error (Internal Server Error).

entry.title: Re: Issue During SAP Certification Exam – Valuation Area...
entry.content:
Hi @PG_Basavachetan 

I reached out to one of our SAP experts and wanted to share the guidance provided—it should help you move forward:

  • Run transaction EC02 and copy an existing plant such as CT00 or 1010.
  • Update the address details for the new plant using OX10.
  • Remove any storage locations that are not required.
  • Verify the activation of Material Ledger via OMX1.
  • Execute CKMSTART to complete the setup.

At this point, most of the configuration should be in place without errors.

Additionally, don’t forget to:

  • Define a Purchasing Organization and assign it to your new plant.
  • Double-check that you are using “0” (zero) instead of the letter “O”, as this can easily cause issues.

Hope this helps—let me know if you have any follow-up questions!

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Re: Managing JMS Queues Programmatically with the new SAP Cloud Integration JMS OData API
entry.content:
@RubikWal 

In some cases (like when a downstream system was down), a large number of messages accumulated in the JMS queue.
When trying to reprocess everything at once, we observed:

  • API timeouts during retry
  • Performance degradation in CPI and target system
  • Difficulty isolating which messages were failing
  • Risk of overwhelming downstream systems

nstead of retrying all messages in one go, we split the backlog based on message creation timestamp (or enqueue time) into smaller chunks.

For example:

  • Retry messages from 10:00–10:10
  • Then 10:10–10:20
  • Then 10:20–10:30

So we process manageable batches instead of full queue at once.

 

  • Prevents system overload
  • Improves retry success rate
  • Makes troubleshooting easier (we know which time range had issues)
  • Gives better control over high-volume recovery

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay: how to correctly configure field format...
entry.content:
Your number for Money fields should be something like this ###,##0.   This brings every entry to a whole number.   There is a setting to round up, down or half up and there is a multiplier option you can set to 1, 10, 100, etc.   For example, if you set the multiplier to 100, you results will always have a value ending in 100.   49,750 would be 49,800 if round up or half up and 49,700 if round down.

The goal section weight is defined in the bonus plan.  you probably have it set to 60 rather than .6.   100 % is configured as 1.

This is all covered in the certification course.   Are you certified in Variable Pay?  If not, you should not be configuring the system without a more experienced colleague.

entry.title: Re: Error in payment proposal creation
entry.content:
Hi Aish,

As other users have stated, without a clear description of your specific case, it is quite hard to provide you with an exact solution. However, I can give you a few general comments and link you to the official documentation. There are several reasons why a payment may fall into an exception. First, it is important to check the payment proposal log, as it details exactly which rule failed. See this SAP Note if you have issues navigating to the log itself: SAP Note 2858351 

Here is a list of the most common exceptions during a payment proposal creation and what they mean in S/4HANA:

  1. 001 - No pymt possible because Items with a debit bal.still exist: Multiple possible reasons, check the SAP Note 2586464 
  2. 003 - Item is Blocked for Payment: This usually means the invoice has a blocking reason (e.g., 'A' or 'R') assigned either manually by a user or automatically by the system (e.g., due to tolerance limits in Invoice Verification). Such an item needs to be "opened" for payment (the block has to be removed). See also SAP Note 3255442 
  3. 006 - No valid payment method found: This might mean: Missing payment method in BP/vendor master, missing bank details, payment method not configured for house bank. See Sap Note 2671294 
  4. 007 - Error in creating the payment document; read job log: Usually a missing G/L account in account determination (e.g. cash discount G/L). See SAP Note 3678208 

Other common exceptions to be aware of:

  1. Vendor blocked, editing not possible: another user is making changes to the same vendor. The locking user should close the transaction to release the lock.
  2. Vendor blocked due to delete flag or posting block: the vendor has a posting block or deletion flag set. This needs to be resolved in the Business Partner master data.
  3. Minimum Amount Not Reached: The total payment amount is lower than the minimum amount defined for the payment method or house bank configuration.

Also, please check the last point in the list on Sap Help - Manage Automatic Payments where other common errors might be listed.

Hope it helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: SAP ASE Internals
entry.content:
@Derek_Asirvadem 

First of all, thanks for posting this really useful info.  There isn't that much like it online anymore.   Now I'll ask some questions about what you're saying:

Re: In Threaded mod, ASE runs as a single (unix) process

In my RedHat/Centos version of Linux, when running ASE in threaded mode, I see this in Linux:

> ps -eLf | egrep "PPID|dataserver" | cut -c1-123 | head -5
UID          PID    PPID     LWP  C NLWP STIME TTY          TIME CMD
syblogin   10056   10043   10056  0   21 May07 ?        00:00:30 /myhomedir/bin/dataserver  
syblogin   10056   10043   13287  0   21 May07 ?        00:05:48 /myhomedir/bin/dataserver  
syblogin   10056   10043   13288  0   21 May07 ?        00:00:03 /myhomedir/bin/dataserver  
syblogin   10056   10043   13289  0   21 May07 ?        00:00:02 /myhomedir/bin/dataserver

So yes, ASE is running as a single process (with PID 10056 in this example), but there are clearly Linux light weight processes (LWP) for each thread in ASE.  I'm pretty sure these LWPs weren't used in the old "process mode" (but I don't have any process mode servers running anymore to check this).

My server has threads configured the following way:

myserver > sp_helpthread;
 Name              Type                 CurrentSize TargetSize IdleTimeout Description                                  InstanceName
 ----------------- -------------------- ----------- ---------- ----------- -------------------------------------------- ------------
 syb_blocking_pool Run To Completion              4          4           0 A pool dedicated to executing blocking calls NULL        
 syb_default_pool  Engine (Multiplexed)           8          8         100 The default pool to run query sessions       NULL        
 syb_system_pool   Run To Completion              3          3           0 The I/O and system task pool                 NULL

So sp_helpthread is showing 15 threads.  Interestingly, at the Linux level, I see 21 threads/LWPs.  Probably a few ASE system threads not showing in sp_helpthread, that's fine.

I generally think of lightweight processes in Linux as OS implemented threads.   From what I'm reading online, threads in Linux are actually processes which happen to share some resources with the parent "thread" (see Light-weight process (Wikipedia))

FYI, you can run "top -H" to see ASE activity at the lightweight thread level.

Re: sp_sysmon stuff

Generally speaking, I was trying to say that sp_sysmon shows things from the engine/thread point of view, not from a user task (spid) point of view.  Ie., you can see that the overall ASE server is busy or now, but it's hard to tell what an individual spid is bottlenecked on.

It's possible to get some idea what individual spids are doing using the "appmgmt" option within sp_sysmon:

> sp_sysmon "00:00:15",appmgmt,@applmon='appl_and_login';
...snip...
  Application->Login:   MyApp->MyLogin

  Application Activity            per sec      per xact       count  % of total
  -------------------------  ------------  ------------  ----------
    CPU Busy                          3.1           0.0          46       5.1 %
    I/O Busy                         57.3           0.0         860      94.5 %
    Idle                              0.3           0.0           4       0.4 %

    Number of Times Scheduled      4170.1           3.2       62552       n/a

  Application Priority Changes    per sec      per xact       count  % of total
  -------------------------  ------------  ------------  ----------  ----------
  Total Priority Changes              0.0           0.0           0       n/a

  Application I/Os Completed     per sec      per xact       count  % of total
  -------------------------  ------------  ------------  ----------  ----------
    Disk I/Os Completed            1143.1           0.9       17146      13.1 %
    Network I/Os Completed         7582.0           5.8      113730      86.9 %
  -------------------------  ------------  ------------  ----------
  Total I/Os Completed             8725.1           6.7      130876

  Resource Limits Violated      per sec      per xact       count  % of total
  -------------------------  ------------  ------------  ----------  ----------
  Total Limits Violated               0.0           0.0           0       n/a
...snip...

Where, according to the docs, CPU, I/O, Idle counts are defined as:

  • CPU Busy – reports the number of clock ticks during which the user task was executing during the sample interval. When the numbers in this category are high, it indicates a CPU-bound application. If this is a problem, you may want to consider engine binding.

  • I/O Busy – reports the number of clock ticks during which the user task was performing I/O during the sample interval. If the numbers in this category are high, it indicates an I/O-intensive process. If idle time is also high, the application could be I/O bound.

    The application might achieve better throughput if you assign it a higher priority, bind it to a lightly loaded engine or engine group, or partition the application’s data onto multiple devices.

  • Idle – reports the number of clock ticks during which the user task was idle during the sample interval.

  • Number of Times Scheduled – reports the number of times a user task is scheduled and chosen to run on an engine. This data can help you determine whether an application has sufficient resources. If this number is low for a task that normally requires substantial CPU time, it may indicate insufficient resources. Consider changing priority in a loaded system with sufficient engine resources.

The way I'm reading this, "Idle" ticks only occur for a spid if it's scheduled.  But if the spid just isn't doing anything at all, then I guess it doesn't get any idle ticks??  That's backed up by observations.    The sum of CPU busy + I/O Busy + Idle ticks doesn't add up to a constant number.

I'm not sure if a spid which is scheduled and waiting for an I/O to complete, shows up as I/O busy ticks or Idle ticks.  Maybe I'll add a separate posting about that.

entry.title: Register now for the 2608 Early Release Series!

These sessions provide an exclusive preview of upcoming innovations, including AI-driven features and advancements with Joule, tailored to selected lines of business and industries. Spanning several days, the series offers expert demos, insider previews, and real-time Q&A, giving participants a first look at exciting updates and the opportunity to get their questions answered directly.

From July 13 to July 17, 2026, we'll have a number of expert-led live sessions taking place to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the 2608 release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you.

_________________

Sign-up instructions:

An active SAP Learning Hub license is required to register for the live sessions and to consume the recordings. Find out how to sign up

As a customer:

Access to at least the SAP Enterprise Support Edition is required. Follow these steps to sign up:

  1. Navigate to the sign-up page and click on the “sign up” button.
  2. Authenticate yourself with your S-user ID and password.
  3. The system will check your eligibility, create a new SAP Learning Hub user, and populate your learning catalogue.
    This process may take up to 2 hours
  4. You will receive a registration confirmation via e-mail once the registration process is complete.

As a partner:
Partners with a valid SAP Universal ID, who are able to login to the Empowering SAP partners site, will be able to access the live sessions and recordings.

As an SAP internal:

Experiencing issues?

entry.title: Re: SAP ASE Internals
entry.content:
Actually, I'll correct/answer myself in the previous post. 

The help doc says I/O busy ticks are for a user task (spid) "performing I/O" (which is not precisely defined), but if a spid also has idle ticks, then it could be I/O bound.

So this seems to say that a spid waiting for an I/O to complete shows up as "idle"

entry.title: Recommendation on Write Variant Date Selection in CV_DVS ILM Archiving
entry.content:
Hi,

Need recommendation on which date field should be used in the write job variant for archiving object CV_DVS when ILM Archiving with Data Destruction option enabled.

In transaction IRM_CUST, under Value Determination for Time References (Indirect), I can see that both ADATUM (Output Date) and VRLDAT (Due Date) from table DRAW are configured by default.

Our requirement is to permanently destroy DMS documents after a retention period of 8 years. Could you please advise which of these date fields should be used as the time reference in the write job variant to correctly calculate the retention expiry date?

Regards,

TY

entry.title: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite is a powerful platform for building and operating integrations at enterprise scale. Its design environment is feature-rich, its runtime is reliable, and its API coverage is broad. But for teams managing dozens — or hundreds — of integration flows across multiple environments, the operational side of the story can quickly become the bottleneck.

Consider a typical scenario: a new release is ready. The team needs to promote integration packages from development to test and then to production. Each package needs its externalized parameters re-applied for the target environment. Log levels need to be set appropriately. Each flow needs to be triggered for activation. If any object is locked, or if the deployment order is wrong, the team has to intervene manually. And when a flow is retired, there is no mechanism to transport that deletion — someone has to remove it by hand in each environment.

The same challenge applies to Partner Directory entries — a critical configuration object for many integration scenarios, especially pipeline-based architectures. Partner Directory entries have no built-in transport mechanism. Setting them up manually on a production system, under time pressure, with no rollback option, is a risk that teams simply should not have to accept.

None of this is a criticism of the platform. It simply reflects what happens when integration teams grow, release frequencies increase, and the margin for error shrinks. Manual processes that work fine for small teams become fragile at scale.

This is the problem that CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite was designed to solve.

What Is It?

CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite is an open-source CI/CD automation layer for SAP Integration Suite, built on GitHub Actions. It was developed jointly by SAP and a customer, born from real operational needs on a large-scale integration landscape. It has been running in production since October 2025 and is available today under the Apache 2.0 license:

https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

A quick note on GitHub Actions — if you are not familiar with it: GitHub Actions is the built-in automation platform of GitHub. It lets you define workflows that run in response to events (a button click, a schedule, or a code push). No separate CI/CD tool to install. The entire deployment pipeline runs directly inside your GitHub repository.

The Core Idea: Git as the Single Source of Truth

The solution is built on one principle: Git holds everything — not just the integration flow source code, but also the configuration that goes with it: externalized parameter values per environment, deployment configuration per flow, Access Policies, and Partner Directory content.

GitHub Actions then uses this Git-stored configuration to fully automate the path from development to production. Deletions travel through the pipeline. Parameter changes are applied automatically. Log levels are managed after deployment. No manual intervention between environments.

SAP Integration Suite — DEV
     │  Download package as ZIP
     │  Extract + merge configuration
     ▼
Git Repository
     │  Change detection
     │  Environment-specific parameters stored as code
     ▼
SAP Integration Suite — TST / PRD / ...
     │  Upload to Design Time
     │  Activate to Runtime
     │  Apply environment-specific log levels
     ▼
Done — fully automated, fully auditable

Every step is recorded in Git. Every deployment is traceable. Every configuration change is reviewed before it is applied.

The Workflow Library at a Glance

The solution ships as a library of reusable GitHub Actions workflows and composite actions. Teams adopt it through a single sync workflow that pulls the latest templates automatically — when new features are released, your workflows update themselves.

 
Environment dropdowns are populated automatically from your configured GitHub Environments. DEV and TST are required with those exact names; any further stages — such as a pre-production or production environment — can be named freely.

Workflow What It Does

Download → Git → DeployDownload an Integration Package or Partner Directory from DEV, store it in Git, deploy to TST
Deploy / DeleteUpload or delete Integration Packages and Partner Directory entries to any configured environment
Delete from BTP and GitRemove an Integration Package or Partner Directory from all environments and from Git
Download to GitSnapshot Integration Packages or Partner Directory entries from any environment to a Git branch
Release ImportCalculate the delta between two Git refs and deploy changed packages to a target environment
Update iFlow ParametersApply externalized parameter changes to a specific iFlow and optionally redeploy
Access Policies (3 workflows)Download, upload, and manage Access Policies across environments
Dashboard GeneratorGenerate the operations dashboard
Create ReleaseVersioned release management from the development branch
 

Every workflow that operates on Integration Packages also supports Partner Directory entries — selected via a single mode input.

What's New in v1.3.0

Version 1.3.0 is the largest release to date.

The Dashboard — See Your Entire Landscape at a Glance

The headline feature is the Integration Suite Operations Dashboard: a static HTML page, automatically generated by a scheduled workflow and published to GitHub Pages. It shows every integration package and every IFlow across all configured environments — runtime status, deployed version, version drift between stages, and whether the package is managed by the pipeline. Color-coded health indicators make problem areas immediately visible.

The dashboard gives an immediate overview of every integration package across all environments — version drift, deployment errors, and unmanaged packages are visible at a glance.

Expanding a package reveals the full detail. Two typical situations a team encounters during release work:

Version drift — both IFlows are running a newer version on DEV and TST than on PRD. Typical during an active release: the new version has been promoted to test but not yet to production. Missing deployment — the second IFlow is configured for production but shows — in the PRD runtime column: it has not been imported yet. The deployment configuration says it should be there; BTP says it is not. Without this dashboard, that gap would likely go unnoticed until a runtime error surfaces.

The dashboard is regenerated daily and can be triggered manually at any time. Self-contained HTML — no server required.

GitHub App Authentication

v1.3.0 replaces Personal Access Tokens with GitHub App authentication. Two dedicated apps handle the read and write sides of the pipeline with minimal, explicit permissions. Token rotation is automatic.

Access Policies as Code

Access Policies can now be version-controlled and promoted through the pipeline. The pattern is identical to integration packages: download from DEV, commit to Git, review, deploy.

Automatic Log Level Management

The deployment pipeline now automatically sets iFlow log levels to an elevated setting before deployment and resets them to the configured operational level afterwards. No manual navigation to the operations cockpit after every release.

A Different Approach to Integration Lifecycle Management

 
SAP provides transport and lifecycle management capabilities for Integration Suite. This solution takes a different path: Git becomes the system of record, GitHub Actions drives the entire lifecycle, and every operation — download, deploy, delete, configure — is automated and auditable. For teams that have adopted a DevOps mindset and want full control over their integration pipeline, this is a deliberate alternative rather than an add-on.

Built Together, Open Sourced Together

This solution was developed collaboratively by SAP and a customer to reduce the manual overhead of release operations on a large enterprise integration landscape. It is available to any team, free of charge, under the Apache 2.0 license, and works on both GitHub Enterprise Cloud and GitHub Enterprise Server.

What's Coming in This Series

  1. The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline — what actually happens, step by step
  2. Managing parameters and log levels — environment-specific configuration as code
  3. The Release Import workflow — controlled promotion using Git tags and branches
  4. Access Policies as code — governance and auditability
  5. The Dashboard in depth — how to read it, configure it, and act on what it shows
  6. Extending the pipeline with Customer Exits — inject your own tests and deployment rules without touching the core

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: Re: ABAP Trial: OData V4 service binding shows Published...
entry.content:
I found the root cause and a confirmed workaround for this issue!
What actually caused the issue:
The root cause is a regression bug introduced in a recent Eclipse ADT update. Normally, in the ABAP Trial environment, clicking "Publish" on an OData V4 Service Binding triggers an automated, hidden background process that exposes the service and registers it in the OData V4 routing tables (like /IWFND/C_V4_MSGR) automatically.
After the latest ADT update, this automation fails. ADT successfully updates the repository object status to "Published", but fails to trigger the server-side runtime registration. This is why older services created before the update work perfectly, while new ones return the /IWBEP/CX_V4_ACCESS_CHECK (404) error.
The Workaround (Step-by-Step):
To bypass this ADT bug, you need to manually force the server-side registration using a Communication Scenario, and then re-trigger the service binding:
  1. Create a Communication Scenario: Right-click your package → New → Other ABAP Repository Object → Communication Scenario (e.g., Z96_FLIGHT_SCENARIO).
  2. Link the Inbound Service: Open the scenario, go to the Inbound tab, click Add..., and select the Inbound Service ID generated by your Service Binding (e.g., ZUI_96FLIGHT_O4_0001_G4BA).
  3. Publish Locally: Click the Publish Locally button in the top-right corner of the editor.
  4. Re-publish the Service Binding: Open your Service Binding again, click Unpublish, wait for it to complete, and then click Publish once more.
After this final re-publishing step, the sync completes properly, and the service endpoint instantly becomes live (HTTP 200) with valid metadata.
Dear SAP ADT Product Management Team / Community Moderators — could you please pass this on to the ADT core tooling developers? It seems the latest Eclipse ADT plugin release broke the automated OData V4 background exposure specific to the ABAP Trial (TRL) shared landscape.

entry.title: Guidance on growth and lifecycle of BW table /BIC/AZADSOPPRS2
entry.content:
Hello SAP Support,

We are analyzing the growth of table /BIC/AZADSOPPRS2 in our SAP BW system. The table currently contains approximately 1.9 million records, and we would like to better understand its standard SAP behavior and any recommended maintenance activities.

Could you please help us clarify the following points?

  1. What is the functional purpose of table /BIC/AZADSOPPRS2 within SAP BW?
  2. Is this table considered a persistent table, or is there any standard SAP mechanism that automatically manages, reduces, or deletes its contents over time?
  3. Is the growth of this table expected under normal operation?
  4. Are there any standard housekeeping, data aging, archiving, or cleanup procedures recommended by SAP for this table?
  5. Would SAP recommend table partitioning or any other optimization strategy when the table reaches this volume?
  6. Are there any SAP Notes, guides, or best practices related to the management and sizing of this table?

Our goal is to understand whether the current table growth is expected and whether any administrative action is required to manage its size and performance.

entry.title: Operationalizing Autonomous CX with the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced Autonomous CX as a core pillar of the Autonomous Enterprise. The principle that every customer promise must be backed by operational reality. The SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience, part of the SAP Services and Support portfolio, is the expert-led engagement model that translates that vision into execution, helping organizations adopt, activate, and scale the SAP CX and AI innovations announced at Sapphire.

The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience is a proactive, expert led engagement model built to derisk transformation, accelerate time to value, and sustain measurable outcomes across CX initiatives. It combines guided adoption, prescriptive functional and technical assistance, AI powered best practices, and continuous value realization aligned to the realities of modern CX: AI at the core, unified data, omnichannel at scale, retention over acquisition, service led growth, and persistent skills gaps in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.

 

At its heart, the plan brings together the right expertise at the right time; program governance, solution experts, value advisors, and adoption specialists. This helps your teams execute faster and smarter with SAP Customer Experience.

 

What Sets the Advanced Success Plan Apart

 

  • Outcome-based: Business outcomes and key value indicators are co-defined with your teams, with milestones and workstreams aligned to deliver measurable Autonomous CX results.
  • Proactive by design: AI Assistants, adoption checks, and innovation accelerators are embedded throughout, reducing risk and compressing time to value as agentic capabilities evolve.
  • Continuous enablement: Role-based enablement and coaching are tied directly to your Autonomous CX roadmap closing skills gaps at pace as new AI and platform capabilities become available.
  • Cross-solution orchestration: Unified processes and shared business context across marketing, commerce, sales and service break silos and enable enterprise-scale execution.

 

This is the first of a summer series where we will go deep on one of the topics below. Today we will start with an introduction on how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience helps you operationalize seven macro trends shaping modern CX.

 

1) AI‑Powered Customer Experiences

AI now underpins everything from next best engagement to intelligent service resolution. The Advanced Success Plan embeds AI adoption patterns directly into your delivery approach; identifying high value use cases, calibrating data prerequisites, and guiding model governance.

The result; Prioritization of high‑impact starting points, a plan to scale with guardrails, accelerating time from pilot to production and grounding every decision in SAP’s CX AI capabilities and product roadmap.

 

 

2) Hyper‑Personalization at Scale

Personalization demands more than algorithms; it requires clean, consent‑aware data, robust decisioning, and experimentation discipline. The Advanced Success Plan delivers:

- Data readiness assessments and integration patterns to enrich customer profiles and segments

- Governance and testing playbooks to validate personalization hypotheses at scale

- Prescriptive journeys to operationalize next best action across every customer channel

The result: hyper personalization moves from proof of concept to standard operating model.

 

3) Unified Customer Data & Breaking Down Silos

Siloed data undermines CX. We help you establish a unified data foundation and harmonized identities, aligning business, data, and integration teams. With technical guidance and adoption accelerators, you can move faster toward a single view of the customer to fuel analytics, personalization, and service excellence:

The result: Unified profile use cases, data quality baselines, and source‑of‑truth decisions to reduce duplication and latency

 

4) Omnichannel Commerce & B2B Digital Transformation

Modern buyers expect seamless journeys across web, mobile, marketplace, and partner portals, especially in B2B. The plan accelerates omnichannel capability build‑out by uniting commerce, order sourcing, pricing, and fulfillment patterns, supported by outcome‑based governance.

The result: Channel consistency, catalog and contract complexity, and the alignment of service and sales motions are all addressed, driving measurable improvement in conversion rates and repeat purchase.

 

5) Customer Retention Over Acquisition

Acquisition costs are rising; retention is the new growth engine. The Advanced Success Plan helps you operationalize retention strategies, churn prediction, intelligent engagement, loyalty, and proactive service across your CX stack.

The result: We align metrics such as retention rate, customer lifetime value, and service‑to‑revenue contribution, and ensure your data foundation supports them.

 

6) Service as a Revenue Driver

Service is no longer a cost center; it’s a growth channel. We guide you to productize services, monetize value‑added offerings, and embed outcome‑based contracts. The plan includes:

- Playbooks for cross‑sell/upsell from service interactions

- Knowledge and field service patterns to improve first‑time fix and attach rates

- KPI frameworks for service‑led growth

The result: With prescriptive governance and AI‑driven intelligence, service organizations move from reactive cost management to consistent, measurable contribution to top‑line revenue and customer retention.

 

7) Navigating Digital Transformation Complexity & Skills Gaps

Large transformation programs falter on orchestration and capability enablement. The Advanced Success Plan addresses both by:

- Establishing a cadence of value sprints and decision forums

- Providing role‑based enablement covering functional and technical assistance, data, product ownership, end-user adoption, and change management

- AI-guided best practices embedded throughout delivery to eliminate rework and accelerate quality outcomes across Industry AI scenarios

 

Organizations execute with confidence — even amid shifting requirements, resource constraints, and rapidly evolving agentic AI capabilities.

 

Measurable Outcomes

- Accelerated time to first value through prioritized, AI-ready use cases aligned to Autonomous CX capabilities

- Higher adoption and sustained performance via continuous enablement

- Reduced program risk through proactive governance, telemetry, and structured decision forums

- Measurable gains in conversion rates, customer retention, and service-led revenue contribution across the full CX stack

 

Getting Started

1) Define your Autonomous CX priorities: Identify two to three priority outcomes for the next two quarters facilitated by the Value Management service.

2) Assess readiness: Evaluate data, integration, governance, and enablement gaps to define a 12–18 month Engagement Plan.

3) Engage the Advanced Success Plan: Align workstreams, milestones, and metrics with our expert team. 

4) Industrialize and scale: Convert proven delivery patterns into reusable accelerators, deployable across regions and lines of business.

 

In the weeks ahead, each edition of this series will examine one of the seven trends in depth. Demonstrating how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience translates CX strategy into repeatable execution and measurable business outcomes.

entry.title: What’s New in the Explore Migration Objects App: Releases 2602.4 and 2608
entry.content:
Explore Migration Objects App: Releases 2602.4 and 2608

The journey continues. Following my first blog post about the Explore Migration Objects app (The New Explore Migration Objects App: The Game Changer in Planning Your Data Migration), new features are now available with release 2602.4 (CFD) and 2608 (Main Release) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

  • New Availability Column for Migration Object
  • New Status Column for Scope Items
  • Enhanced Dependency Graph

These features may be small additions, but they bring meaningful improvements. They primarily focus on the availability of migration objects. As most of us know, migration objects are assigned to solution scenarios, as SAP Signavio Process Navigator refers to them, or scope items, which is the more commonly used term in the context of SAP Best Practices.

Depending on the activation status of a scope item, a migration object will either appear or not appear in the Migration Cockpit and can therefore be used or excluded from data migration accordingly. This behavior is very useful as it ensures you only see the migration objects that are actually needed to run your business processes. Beyond scope item activation, there are additional visibility restrictions for data migration objects. A data migration object can be linked to a feature toggle or depend on a business function activation, which determines whether it is visible or not.

But lets start with the first new feature: the migration object availability status.

New Availability Colum for Migration Objects

As mentioned earlier, migration objects always depend on the activated scope. If a specific business solution or scope item is not activated, the related migration objects will also unavailable , unless they are assigned to another activated business process . 

A new "Availability" column has been added to the Migration Objects view , giving you a clearer overview of which of the hundreds of existing migration objects  are available in your system based on your activated scope. 

You can also use the new Quick Filter  to narrow down the list of migration objects by availability status:

  • All
  • Availability: Yes
  • Availability: No

The Quick Filter also displays the number of migration objects per availability status.

The result list in Figure 1 shows the status within the "Availability" column . Depending on the status, you immediately see whether an object is available:

  • Yes - Object is available based on the activated scope
  • No - Object is not available to be used
  • (!) Yes - The object may be available if a specific condition is met. You might need to activate a feature to make it visible.  Hover over the entry to open an information window that includes a link to the object's documentation, where you can find further details

Migration Object Availability Status on Selection Screen

Figure 1:  Migration Object Availability Status on Selection Screen

In the example above, you can see that out of 240 migration objects, 23 are not available at all.

By selecting an object you will be directed to the "Migration Object" dependency graph where availability is displayed directly within the migration object's node as one of its attributes,  highlighted in yellow in Figure 2 below.

Migration Object Availability Status in Dependency Graph

Figure 2:  Migration Object Availability Status in Dependency Graph

Similar to the migration object availability, it is also often useful to see which objects are listed for the activated scope. 

New Status Column for Scope Items

The "Status" column on the "Scope Items" view  (see Figure 3) shows the activation status of a specific business process or scope item.  A Quick Filter option allows you to display All, Active or Inactive scope items including  the exact count per status.

Scope Item Activation Status on Selection Screen

Figure  3:  Scope Item Activation Status on Selection Screen

In the Scope items graph,  the activation status is displayed in the header area. In our example, we have selected an inactive scope item for a Retail solution scenario (see Figure 4).

Scope Item base Dependency Graph incl. Availability Status

Figure 4:  Scope Item base Dependency Graph incl. Availability Status

Enhanced Dependency Graph

Based on user feedback and the product roadmap the dependency graph has been enhanced with three additional features:

  • Filter Available Only Migration Objects
  • Group/Ungroup Migration objects
  • Additional Node Attributes

Filter by Availability Status

The first enhancement improves the visibility of complex objects or dependency graphs with multiple relations. Using the new Quick Filter, you can filter migrations objects by availability status and choose to display either "All" objects or only those  with "Availability: Yes". The can significantly reduce visual complexity and improve clarity.  As shown in Figure 5, the scope item example from above now displays only the available, while others are hidden.

Availability Quick Filter on Dependency Graph View

Figure 5:  Availability Quick Filter on Dependency Graph View

Group/Ungroup Function

The next very useful enhancement is the new Ungroup/Group button. By default objects are grouped by their development area domain, which is particularly helpful in the Scope Items view as it gives you a clear picture of where each migration object belongs. For example, Figure 4 shows the scope item along with all its available objects. You can see that the available objects belongs to "Classification" domain, whereas the unavailable RFM objects belong to the "Logistics Basic Data" domain.

Another example  is a complex migration object such as "SD - Sales oder (only open SO)".  In Figure 6 you can see the Sales Order object, which belongs to "Sales and Distribution" domain and has numerous relationships to objects in other development domains. Note that the view is already pre-filtered to show only the available migration objects.

Complex Migration Object that is Grouped

 Figure 6: Complex Grouped Migration object

If you click on Ungroup button, the grouping is removed and the view changes to a much simpler layout that takes up less screen and is therefore easier to read (see Figure 7).

Complex Ungrouped Migration Object

 Figure 7: Ungrouped Complex Migration Object 

Additional Node Attributes

As mentioned earlier  the migration object node now includes the new "Availability" status attribute . In addition, if an object is not available, an exclamation mark icon is displayed in the upper right corner of the node.

New Node Attribute Availability Status

Figure 8: New Node Attribute Availability Status 

What’s Next

The enhancements described in this blog post are just the beginning. There are more exciting features already on the roadmap for upcoming releases and CFDs that will further improve the Explore Migration Objects app and simplify your data migration planning. Stay tuned for future blog posts where I will cover these new capabilities as they become available. In the meantime, I encourage you to explore the new features in your system and share your feedback with the SAP community.

entry.title: Re: SAP BAS Deployment Issue - Request for Support
entry.content:
Dear Pranamr and team,

Per my research internally in SAP, and the error attached:
- The HTTP 404 issue may be tied to the Fiori add deploy-config.  When the team selects ABAP as the target, the tool immediately attempts to reach out to the backend or a destination mapping to fetch the service information.  This is a network request failure with a standard "Request failed with status 404".
- The ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE error caused a crash.  Instead of handling the 404 error gracefully, the internal error-handling script of the tool exited and processed the process.exit(code), but passes the text string 'ERR_BAD_REQUEST' instead of an integer like 1.  <-- the node.js strictly expects a number here causing the CLI tool itself to crash. 

So suggestion to run the @SAP/generator-fiori may not be the correct suggestion as it will not fix the underlying network issue.

So due to this is an endpoint type of issue (network), the team has to figure out the 404 as that means the tool cannot find the ABAP system endpoint through the configuration BTP destination in BAS.

Next Steps:

  1. - So verify the BTP Destination endpoint that the ABAP system is correctly configured in the SAP BTP Cockpit.
  2. - Ensure that the destination contains the properties of the SAP BAS web IDE usage like: WebIDEUsage=odata_abap, WebIDEEnabled = true
  3. If connecting to an on-premise ABAP Backend via the SAP Cloud Connector (SCC), verify that the cloud connector is active and that the resource (specifically the path to the ABAP ADT/Odata service) are allowed (SAP Component:  BC-MID-SCC)
  4. Additional note:  If after the above, the team get past the 404 network issue but the SAP Cloud Connector hits a size limitation -> Hit up SAP note 2875647 - https://me.sap.com/notes/2875647 - SAPUI5 ABAP Repository Load via OData: URL parameters to reduce size of messages in HTTP response header

Other ideas:  Use the modern deploy or ui5-deploy path.

- Since CLI tool is crashing on an argument type mismatch, it is possible that the outdated or mistmatch version of the local UX tools may also influence it.  Try doing a refresh.

---BAS Terminal----

# Clean out cached npm data
npm cache clean --force

# Re-install clean project dependencies based on the package.json
npm install
----end-----------

- According to the SAP BAS development support or legacy-deployment tools (abap-deploy) are no longer in active development

  • There are references here:  
    • In order to deploy an application from Business Application Studio to ABAP system, our recommendation is to use "deploy" command as
      specified here:
      https://help.sap.com/viewer/17d50220bcd848aa854c9c182d65b699/Latest/en-US/1b7a3be8d99c45aead90528ef472af37.html  

These are what I researched internally.

May this assist or other team members may also share what they know about the topic. 

Have a great day.

Regards,
Mark


-

entry.title: Refining Diagnostics in SAP for Me Services & Support Dashboard
entry.content:
Supporting Early Risk Detection with SAP EarlyWatch Alert

Preventing system issues and anticipating potential risks are key goals when operating SAP landscapes—on‑premise, cloud, and hybrid—today. With the refined Diagnostics tab in SAP for Me (Services & Support Dashboard), SAP provides a clearer and more focused entry point into SAP EarlyWatch Alert–based system insights.

The current experience represents an important first step: simplifying access to system health information while establishing a foundation for future enhancements that further support early risk identification and forward‑looking system management.

 

A Refined Entry Point — Not a New One

The Diagnostics tab itself is not new; what has changed is its focus, structure, and clarity.
The former Diagnostics, Alerts and Analysis tab has been streamlined and renamed to Diagnostics putting greater emphasis on clear system health visibility and actionable SAP EarlyWatch Alert insights. It offers:

  • Dedicated  system health visibility Integration card
  • Quick access to main capabilities (Solution Finder, Reports, Dashboard, Status) and forecast applications
  • One-click subscription for notifications.

This refinement marks an initial milestone in an ongoing journey—supporting customers in moving beyond reactive analysis toward a more preventive and forward‑looking approach, enabled by SAP EarlyWatch Alert insights.

System Health as the Starting Point

When opening the refined Diagnostics tab, customers are presented with a clean and consolidated overview highlights the number of systems per status category in his landscape.

The System Health Status is displayed

  • as a pie chart, and
  • as a numerical summary

Systems are categorized into:

  • Very Critical – systems with a critical overall status (based on decisive red findings)
  • Critical – systems with relevant issues reflected in their overall status
  • OK – systems without critical findings
  • Incomplete or outdated data – systems with missing data or reports older than 14 days.

From Overview to Detail: SAP EarlyWatch Alert Workspace

Access to detail is essential when addressing system risks. 

By clicking on the system health chart, customers are taken directly into the SAP EarlyWatch Alert Workspace, where they continue their analysis in their user‑specific default view with:

  • review findings in detail
  • identify recurring or systemic issues, and
  • initiate corrective actions at an early stage.

Notifications: Stay informed

The Diagnostics tab also provides an option one click option to subscribe for notifications.

Available notifications inform customers about: 

  • changes in system health (e.g. new Decisive Alerts)
  • newly identified critical findings (e.g. Server Slowdowns) 
  • critical forecasts (e.g. SAP HANA Table Records)

This reduces the need for manual checks and supports timely intervention before issues escalate.

Quick Access: SAP EarlyWatch Alert main applications

  • SAP EarlyWatch Solution Finder
  • SAP EarlyWatch Alert Reports
  • SAP EarlyWatch Alert Dashboard
  • SAP EarlyWatch Alert Status

Forecast Applications: Looking Ahead

In addition to the current system health view, the Diagnostics tab provides direct access to selected Forecast applications. These applications represent the predictive capabilities of SAP EarlyWatch Alert and help customers to:

  • recognize data over time,
  • assess upcoming capacity or stability risks, and
  • take action before limits are reached.

By placing these applications directly on the Diagnostics tab, SAP for Me makes forward‑looking insights more visible—especially for customers who do not work in the SAP EarlyWatch Alert Workspace on a daily basis.

Available Forecast applications include:

  • SAP HANA Table Records
  • Number Ranges
  • SAP HANA Indexserver: Main Memory Usage

Designed to Evolve

The refined Diagnostics experience represents the starting point of a broader evolution.

By consolidating system health indicators, SAP EarlyWatch insights, notifications, and forecast access into a single entry point, SAP establishes a scalable foundation for deeper insights and closer integration across diagnostics capabilities.

Conclusion

By refining the former “Diagnostics, Alerts and Analysis” tab and focusing it under Diagnostics, SAP for Me delivers:

  • a clearer and more intuitive entry point
  • immediate visibility into overall system health
  • seamless reuse of existing SAP EarlyWatch Alert views
  • timely notifications, and
  • easy access to forward‑looking forecast insights.

The current state is only the beginning—but an important one—supporting customers in staying ahead of potential system issues.

Explore the refined diagnostics experience

Log in to SAP for Me and take a look at the evolving Diagnostics tab.

Other blogs you might be interested in

  • 🎉Celebrating the Successful Launch of Joule in SAP for Me
  • SAP EarlyWatch Alert: UI Refresh for Improved Readability and Usability

entry.title: Re: Workflow approval history no longer visible

entry.title: Re: SAP BAS Deployment Issue - Request for Support
entry.content: Per R3nwo comment: Hii, I am facing the same problem. My workaround was that I used the UI wizard (press F1 and search for Deployment) to create the deploy config. Basically, the UI Wizard bypasses the exact - text based prompt mechanism and the specific Type Error [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TARGET] crash happens because the node.js process in the terminal chokes on how the error return string 'ERR_BAD_REQUEST'. The Graphical UI extension handles the state management and backend request via the IDE's build-in VS Code/BAS platform API, which process and responds completely different that do not trigger this text-argument routine. So per R3nwo, this UI wizard has direct active access to the session's BTP space and authentication without manually bridging an external terminal process.

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Central Business Configuration 2604 FD1
entry.content:
With the latest release for SAP Central Business Configuration, an exciting new feature is available. This blog post will provide you with a comprehensive overview of it.

Partner Add-On Showcase Tenants Now Available with Demo Data

Add-on developers will benefit from a new option in the Deployment Target activity. By default, the Partner Add-On Showcase tenants come with demo data now. If needed, you can switch off demo data before assignment. If SAP implementation partners have the required license, they can assign the following deployment targets to their evaluation workspaces, with or without demo data:

  • Partner Add-On Showcase Development Tenant
  • Partner Add-On Showcase Customizing Tenant

Demo data selection in the Assign Deployment Target activity

For a quick summary, check out this video about what’s new. Don’t forget to also use the What’s New Viewer on the SAP Help Portal, which provides further details and links about all the recent changes.

That wraps up the new features this time. Did you like this blog? If so, please leave us a thumbs up at the top. Also, make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Do you have any ideas on how to further enhance SAP Central Business Configuration? Go to the Customer Influence platform to submit an improvement request or vote for the ideas of others. This helps us understand your requirements even better and prioritize our work accordingly. We’re looking forward to your improvement requests.

entry.title: Re: SAP HANA and Hadoop Integration: ETL vs. SDA vs. SDI vs. HANA Vora
entry.content:
Current Environment and Request for Recommendations

We currently maintain two SAP HANA 2.x instances that support our Development/QA and Production environments.

Our data integration process relies on SAP Data Services to connect remotely to an Oracle database. We regularly receive Oracle Data Pump export files containing data warehouse tables sourced from HR systems. These exports are imported into an intermediate Oracle database, which serves as a bridge between the source system and SAP HANA.

To load data into SAP HANA, we currently use a combination of third-party PL/SQL-based processes and SAP HANA import utilities. Large fact tables are loaded through specialized processes, while small and medium-sized tables (typically less than one million rows) are loaded using bulk insert operations. Data is refreshed and loaded into SAP HANA six days per week.

In addition to scheduled loads, we frequently receive requests from business offices to load additional data into SAP HANA for reporting purposes. We use a SAP HANA import tool to load the data and then perform transformations and reformatting as needed. Stored procedures are extensively used to complete data transformations, load data into SAP HANA, and generate output files that are shared with other organizations in text file format.

One of our most cumbersome tasks involves processing files received in PDF and Excel (.xlsx) formats. These files must be manually reformatted into delimited text files before they can be processed by our SAP HANA import tool. This manual effort is time-consuming and prone to errors.

We are also experiencing performance challenges with our daily Oracle-to-SAP HANA data migration process. The volume of data continues to grow at approximately one gigabyte per year, and the current migration process takes approximately four hours to complete. The workflow currently involves exporting data from the source Oracle database, importing it into an intermediate Oracle database, and then loading it into SAP HANA. This multi-step process introduces complexity and additional processing time.

We are seeking recommendations to modernize and redesign our architecture. Specifically, we would like guidance on:

  1. Leveraging SAP HANA-native tools to load and transform data more efficiently.
  2. Eliminating unnecessary intermediate technologies and reducing the dependency on the bridge Oracle database.
  3. Identifying ETL solutions that can automate the conversion of PDF, Excel, and other file formats into SAP HANA-ready data structures.
  4. Improving overall load performance and reducing processing time.
  5. Supporting the ingestion and processing of unstructured data and streaming data sources.
  6. Incorporating Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning capabilities into our data platform.
  7. Establishing a scalable architecture that can support future growth and increasing data volumes.

We would appreciate recommendations for an end-to-end redesign that simplifies the current architecture, improves performance, reduces manual effort, and positions the platform for future AI and advanced analytics initiatives.

entry.title: Testing Data Flows in S/4HANA Migrations: Why SIT is Your Data Quality Filter
entry.content:
Introduction

If you are a data engineer, you probably spend months building perfect pipelines and setting strict governance rules. But how do you know they actually work when systems start talking to each other? That is what System Integration Testing (SIT) is for.

Think of SIT as the first time we turn on the switches and let data flow end-to-end across different platforms. It is not just a software check; it is the ultimate stress-test to see if your clean data stays clean when it leaves your pipeline and enters a live business environment.

When executing System Integration Testing (SIT), teams often fall into the trap of only validating the business process—checking if system A successfully passes a message to system B. But a successful technical handshake doesn't mean the data itself is sound. This is where the critical angle of data quality and integrity comes in; to prove that, let us first remember the definitions of data quality and data integrity.

Data quality definition

Data quality refers to how relevant and reliable your data is for its intended purpose. It defines whether information can be trusted and effectively applied in daily operations or advanced data analytics. True data quality also depends on preserving business semantics, which are the shared definitions, context, and meaning behind the data.

And what is Data Integrity

is about ensuring that data remains accurate, complete, and consistent as it moves across systems throughout its lifecycle. It’s not just about a single field looking right; it’s about maintaining the logical relationships, financial meaning, and correctness of the data as it flows from one system to another.

Process vs. Data

If a pipeline moves information from one system to another without crashing, a basic technical check will mark the test as "passed."

But what is actually inside that data?

This is where true data integrity comes in. Basic data quality only checks if a field looks right — like making sure a phone number has enough digits. Data integrity goes deeper than that. It's about whether the data keeps its real meaning and logic as it moves from system to system. During SIT, you'll almost always find problems that simple checks completely miss: empty fields, cut-off text, duplicate records that suddenly appear right after a migration load. If your tests only check whether the data moved, you're missing the more important information — whether it is actually usable or not.

The SAP Chain Reaction: A Lesson for Data Teams.

For data engineers, in SAP projects, weak data rules turn visible very fast, and the consequences spread quickly.

Take a simple buying flow as an example. Data moves from a legacy system into SAP Materials Management (MM), then passes into Financial Accounting (FI). If a tax code or currency field is missing in the master data, the system might still let the Goods Receipt post in MM without any errors, but the moment that same data hits the FI module, everything stops — the accounts don't balance, and the transaction fails.

This is the part that trips up a lot of teams. When a transaction fails in SIT, the first instinct is to raise it as a software bug and log a ticket. More often than not, though, it's a data governance gap hiding behind a technical error. The system didn't break — the data was never ready to begin with. For data teams, these failures show you exactly where ownership and validation rules are missing, often more clearly than any audit ever could.

A Live Feedback Loop for Data Engineers

Data engineers shouldn't wait until System Integration Test to find out their pipelines have holes in them — but SIT is usually where it happens anyway. So the smarter move is to treat it as exactly what it is: a live report card.

When you transform legacy data into a new SAP structure, SIT is the environment that proves whether your transformation logic holds up under real business transactions — not just in a dev sandbox, but end-to-end. For data scientists especially, seeing broken or missing data at this stage is a critical early warning. If a pipeline is letting bad data through now, any dashboard or model built on top of it will produce bad output. It just quietly makes your analytics wrong until someone notices.

SIT catches these broken pipelines early. That's the point. Long before they cause damage in production.

Conclusion: Working Together for Clean Data

No matter how well a system is configured, it will stall if the data running through it isn't clean. Getting successfully to UAT requires data creators and data validators to actually work together — not hand off a ticket and move on.

SIT shouldn't be treated as a way to patch quick errors and close testing tickets. When a data issue surfaces, engineers and testers need to sit together and trace it back to the root cause in the pipeline — not just fix the symptom. That's the difference between clearing a defect and actually building something reliable.

References & Further Reading

  • SAP PRESS Book: SAP S/4HANA Cloud: An Introduction (Chapter on Data Migration Workstreams and SAP Activate Testing Methodology).
  • Industry Standard Framework: The DAMA Guide to the Data Management Body of Knowledge (DAMA-DMBOK) – Section on Data Quality and Integration Governance.
  • SAP Community Guide: Data Migration in SAP S/4HANA – The Core Principles of Data Cleansing during the Realize Phase.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
entry.content:
@mandli_girish 

Compute is consumed for data merging, optimizing, vacuuming, transformation flows, and all other tasks that change data. This means for frequent data change + frequent replication needs then this pattern is not ideal.  

Ideal scenarios:

  • High volume + Low/moderate update.
  • Mostly batch processing (ingest → transform → aggregate) i.e. every 1hr, 4hr, daily etc.

Inefficient when:

  • Small and frequent updates to high volume tables.

cold‑first is efficient when mostly append‑only and batch‑processed.

Hope this help. Happy learning, Jeet
